title: Jokes or Gibberish? Humor Retention in Translation with Neural Machine Translation vs. Large Language Model
research_questions: How well do different translation models (MT, GPT, GPT-P, and GPT-Ex) preserve humor in English-to-Thai joke translation?
What are the most common humor loss patterns in machine-translated jokes?

methods_proposed.name: GPT-P
methods_proposed.summary: A humor-preserving prompt for GPT-4o that instructs the model to recognize humor such as wordplay, puns, and cultural references and adapt the translation naturally for Thai speakers while avoiding explanations.

methods_proposed.name: GPT-Ex
methods_proposed.summary: A humor-preserving GPT-4o prompt that additionally asks the model to provide an explanation in parentheses after the Thai translation, clarifying wordplay, double meanings, or cultural references when needed.

humor_theories.detail: The related work states that humor arises from unexpected contrasts in meaning and that humor translation relies on incongruity resolution, intention attribution, and cultural knowledge.
The related work mentions Superiority Theory as framing humor as deriving from perceived dominance over others.

The paper manually classifies jokes with a multi-label scheme—Wordplay, Idioms, Cultural Reference, Irony, Dark Humor, Absurd Humor, and Other—following prior work on linguistic vs. cultural humor in translation, taxonomies of linguistic jokes, and computational humor frameworks.

evaluation_methods.detail: Three Thai-native annotators manually categorized each translated joke as X (Not Understandable), XJ (Recognized as a Joke but Not Understood), J (Joke Understood), or N (Not a Joke); final labels were assigned by majority vote, with consensus discussion or blinded adjudication for 1–1–1 splits.

Humor retention was defined as the percentage of translations labeled J; the paper also reports full class distributions, exact pairwise McNemar tests on J vs. non-J, paired percentage-point gaps, win–loss ratios, shift heatmaps, and stratified retention by joke type.
Annotator reliability was reported as Fleiss’ Kappa κ = 0.6536 and simple agreement percentage 78.57% after written bilingual guidelines, oral explanations, and a 15-item test set.

headline_results.finding: GPT-Ex had the highest overall humor retention, followed by GPT-P, MT, and baseline GPT.
GPT-Ex significantly increased humor retention over MT.
Baseline GPT significantly decreased humor retention relative to MT.
GPT-P was numerically higher than MT, but the difference was not statistically significant at α = 0.05.
For wordplay, GPT-Ex showed a statistically robust improvement and the paper reports a large retention increase over baseline GPT.
headline_results.value: GPT 32.20% to GPT-Ex 59.81% (+27.61 percentage points); MT vs. GPT-Ex n01 = 113, n10 = 44, p = 3.5 × 10^{-8}

key_findings: Only GPT-Ex clearly outperformed MT overall: GPT-Ex achieved 62.94% retention versus MT’s 50.12%, while GPT-P reached 54.12% and baseline GPT only 41.06%.
Baseline GPT was significantly worse than MT, with ΔJ = −9.06 percentage points and exact McNemar p = 1.19 × 10^{-8}.
GPT-P improved numerically over MT by +4.00 percentage points, but this did not reach statistical significance at α = 0.05 (p = 0.0649).

The paper identifies two dominant baseline-GPT failure modes among jokes originally labeled J in MT: 45 cases (10.56%) became Not Understandable and 85 cases (19.95%) became Not a Joke, for 130 failed jokes (30.52%).

Stratified McNemar tests confirmed significant GPT-Ex gains over MT for Wordplay, Cultural Reference, Irony, and Dark Humor; Idioms, Absurd, and Other were not statistically significant because of small sample sizes.
The annotator protocol achieved Fleiss’κ = 0.6536 and 78.57% simple agreement, which the paper describes as moderate to substantial agreement and relatively high consistency.
limitations: The annotation protocol did not include an explicit complexity baseline, difficulty ratings, or calibrated easy/medium/hard exemplars.
Some joke-type categories had small sample sizes, especially Idioms (n = 24) and Absurd Humor (n = 28), limiting statistical power and making those results exploratory.
The study focuses on English-to-Thai joke translation, so the reported findings are tied to that language pair and its linguistic and cultural challenges.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper notes that some jokes in the Humorous Jokes Dataset may contain offensive content; the authors declare no conflict of interest.

artifacts.other: The data presented in this study are available on request from the corresponding author.

human_samples.observations: Independently labeled translated joke outputs into X, XJ, J, or N; final labels were set by majority vote with consensus/adjudication for 1-1-1 splits. The study used 850 English jokes translated by four systems.
human_samples.detail: Training used written bilingual guidelines and oral explanations, followed by a 15-item test set with feedback. Reported inter-rater reliability on pre-consensus labels: Fleiss’ κ = 0.6536 and simple agreement = 78.57%. No recruitment, compensation, age, gender, location, or translation/humor expertise details are reported beyond Thai-native status.

human_samples.population: Manual classifiers of the original English jokes; the paper states that 'we' applied the scheme but does not identify the classifiers or count them.
human_samples.observations: Manually assigned multi-label joke-type categories to the 850 source jokes: Wordplay n=413, Idioms n=24, Cultural Reference n=203, Irony n=224, Dark Humor n=177, Absurd Humor n=28, Other n=47. Counts are category memberships and overlap because the scheme is multi-label.
human_samples.detail: Classification was based on structural and semantic cues in the original English jokes. The paper does not report coder number, coder qualifications, independence, adjudication procedure, or reliability for this joke-type labeling.

judges.detail: Three Thai-native annotators independently categorized each translated text as X, XJ, J, or N. The paper does not report whether annotators were blinded to translation system or prompt condition. Majority-vote/consensus/adjudicated labels are the primary basis for humor-retention results.

For unresolved 1-1-1 splits after annotator consensus discussion, the protocol says a blinded adjudicator assigned the final label. The paper does not report the adjudicator’s qualifications or the number of items adjudicated.

The source jokes were manually categorized into overlapping humor-type labels by the authors/research team as implied by 'we applied a multi-label scheme'; exact labelers and reliability are not reported.

judges.name: Humor retention rate and exact McNemar tests
judges.detail: Humor retention was computed as the percentage of final human labels equal to J. Pairwise significance used exact McNemar tests on J vs. non-J discordant pairs; full class distributions and shift heatmaps were descriptive.

generation_selection.scope: Google Translate / MT translations for 850 English jokes
generation_selection.detail: The study reports one MT translation per source joke evaluated by annotators. No best-of-N generation, alternative candidates, or selection step is reported.

generation_selection.scope: GPT-4o prompt conditions: baseline GPT, GPT-P, and GPT-Ex
generation_selection.n: 1 evaluated output per joke per prompt condition
generation_selection.detail: Each GPT prompt condition produced the evaluated Thai translation; GPT-Ex additionally instructed the model to provide a parenthetical explanation. No multiple-candidate generation or output-selection procedure is reported.

model_provenance.name: Google Translate
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: deep_translator Python package version 1.11.4; Google Translate NMT system as of January 2025
model_provenance.detail: Used as the MT condition via the deep_translator package. The exact Google Translate backend/model version or service snapshot is not reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Used for baseline GPT, GPT-P, and GPT-Ex prompt conditions. The paper gives the model name and system prompts but does not report an API snapshot/version, endpoint, access route, or inference date.

inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports use of Google Translate via deep_translator but does not report batching, retries, call counts, rate limits, cost, or any service parameters.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-4o baseline GPT, GPT-P, and GPT-Ex conditions
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 evaluated output per source joke per prompt condition
inference_budgets.detail: System prompts are reported, but max tokens, temperature, top_p, seed, stop conditions, retries, batching, latency, and cost are not reported.

human_baselines.description: No human translator output or human translation-performance baseline was evaluated. Humans served as annotators of machine-produced translations, not as a matched translation baseline.
human_baselines.detail: The study compares Google Translate with GPT-4o prompt variants only.

contamination_risk.basis: The 850 source jokes were randomly selected from the public Kaggle Humorous Jokes Dataset, cited as a 2020 Kaggle competition and sourced from public websites/social media. The evaluated systems include Google Translate as of January 2025 and GPT-4o, so the source jokes were public before the model runs and plausibly before/within proprietary model training data. The paper provides no decontamination analysis, held-out/private item construction, or controls for model prior exposure. Risk is moderated by the fact that the evaluated outputs and Thai humor-retention labels were newly produced for this study, but the public legacy joke source keeps contamination risk at least moderate.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Primary evaluation labels came from three Thai-native human annotators; no LLM-as-evaluator is reported.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLMs were used as translation generators, not as judges or automatic evaluators. Statistical conclusions are computed from human labels.

reporting_gaps: No GPT-4o API snapshot, version date, endpoint, access method, or inference date is reported.
No exact Google Translate backend/model snapshot is reported beyond deep_translator package version 1.11.4 and 'as of January 2025'.
No GPT sampling or decoding parameters are reported, including temperature, top_p, max tokens, seed, stop conditions, retries, or batching.
No model call counts, retry policy, latency, compute, or monetary cost are reported for either Google Translate or GPT-4o conditions.
No annotator recruitment method, compensation, demographics, geography, bilingual proficiency, or humor/translation expertise is reported beyond Thai-native status.
The paper states that it reports the number of adjudicated items, but the supplied text does not provide that number.
The number of 1-1-1 splits, consensus-discussion cases, and unresolved cases sent to the blinded adjudicator is not reported.
The source and construction of the 15-item test set’s gold-standard labels are not described.
Manual source-joke type classification lacks coder count, coder identity, independence/blinding, disagreement resolution, and inter-coder reliability reporting.
No human translator baseline is included.
No contamination or decontamination analysis is reported for the public Kaggle/online joke dataset.
The data are stated to be available on request; no public release of the translated outputs, human labels, or analysis code is reported.
